banner.development: This project is in active development. Versions 0.x may be unstable.

hero.title: Kirkouski Typographic
hero.subtitle: Em dashes, curly quotes, Polish ogonki (ą ć ę ł ń ó ś ź ż), and 80+ typographic symbols — all on AltGr.

legend.deadKeyTip: Press dead key, then a letter to produce an accented character (e.g. ´ then a → á)

playback.reducedMotionHint: Animation off — click "Play demo" to start.

russian: Беларуская мова: і, ў — вось і ўсё!

windows.title: Fewer input languages
windows.problem: On Windows, every keyboard layout is tied to an input language. To type Polish characters you have to add Polish as an input language; to type Russian you add Russian. The Win+Space switcher then cycles through everything you've added — and the more languages, the worse the toggle.
windows.case1: {en} + Polish typing = 2 input languages with the standard Polish layout. With US+POL, just {en} (1 input language).
windows.case2: {en} + {ru} + Polish typing = 3 input languages with the standard Polish layout. With US+POL, only {en} + {ru} (2 input languages).
windows.solution: The US+POL Typographic layout registers under English (US) instead of Polish — Polish characters live on AltGr, English stays your system language, and the Win+Space cycle stays short.

typography.title: Typography on the keyboard
typography.lead: Real typographic characters at your fingertips, not Unicode-picker excavations:
typography.symbols: Euro, pound, copyright, arrows, math symbols — all on AltGr
typography.note: No compose key, no autocorrect rule to maintain, no copy-pasting from Character Map.

download.title: Download
download.subtitle: Get the layout for your system

exe.step1: Download and run the .exe installer — it handles everything automatically and prompts for a reboot
exe.step2: Restart when prompted — the new layout will appear in Settings after the reboot
exe.step3: After the reboot, go to Settings > Time & Language > Language & Region > Keyboard and add the new layout
exe.uninstall: To uninstall: open Settings > Apps > Installed Apps, find Kirkouski Typographic, and click Uninstall. Reboot required.

zip.step1: Download the .zip, extract it, right-click install.ps1 and select "Run with PowerShell"
zip.step2: RESTART YOUR COMPUTER before using the new layout. The script will prompt you. Don't switch to the Kirkouski Typographic layout in Win+Space until after the reboot — Explorer will crash if the DLL isn't loaded yet.
zip.step3: After the reboot, go to Settings > Time & Language > Language & Region > Keyboard and add the new layout
zip.uninstall: To uninstall: run install.ps1 again — it detects the install and offers removal. Reboot required.

windows.loginScreen: To apply to login screen: Win+R > intl.cpl > Administrative > Copy settings > check both boxes
windows.hardCleanup: If Explorer crashes after a reboot (rare): run {cmd} to remove all traces.

pkg.step1: Download and run the .pkg installer — it copies the keyboard bundle to the correct location automatically
pkg.step2: Log out and log back in (a full logout, not just the lock screen)
pkg.step3: Open System Settings > Keyboard > Input Sources > + and add the Polish/Russian Kirkouski Typographic layouts

zip.step1: Download the .zip, unzip, and move Kirkouski Typographic.bundle to {userPath} (current user) or {systemPath} (all users)
zip.step2: Clear the quarantine xattr — without this, macOS may silently refuse to load the bundle: {cmd}
zip.step3: Log out and log back in (a full logout, not just the lock screen)
zip.step4: Open System Settings > Keyboard > Input Sources > + and add the Polish/Russian Kirkouski Typographic layouts

about.title: Based on
about.baseline: Built on Polish Programmers QWERTY and Russian ЙЦУКЕН — same symbols in the same positions across all variants.
about.license: Open source under the MIT License.
about.starHint: If you find this useful, a star helps others discover it

items.q: How do I type an em dash on a Polish keyboard in Windows?
items.a: Press AltGr + - (minus key). The Kirkouski Typographic layout puts em dash (—), en dash (–), and other typographic symbols directly on AltGr — no Alt codes needed.

items.q: How do I type Polish characters on a US keyboard without switching layouts?
items.a: Install the US+POL Typographic variant. It registers under English (US) so Win+Space stays clean — Polish ogonki (ą ć ę ł ń ó ś ź ż) are on AltGr.

items.q: Does this work with Polish Programmers QWERTY?
items.a: Yes. The Polish variant is built on the standard Polish Programmers QWERTY layout — all base and shift keys are identical. Typographic symbols are added on AltGr and Shift+AltGr layers only.

items.q: Will this add a new input language to Win+Space?
items.a: The US+POL variant does not — it registers under English (US). The standard Polish and Russian variants do add their respective input language, as Windows requires.
Yes. Download the .bundle, place it in ~/Library/Keyboard Layouts/, clear the quarantine xattr, and log out/in. macOS keyboard layouts are not tied to system language.

items.q: What is the difference between this and Birman's Typography Layout?
items.a: This layout adds Polish diacritics (ą ć ę ł ń ó ś ź ż) on AltGr and repositions some symbols for a more consistent Polish/Russian experience. The typographic symbol positions are compatible with Birman's layout.

fonts.title: Pronunciation Fonts
fonts.subtitle: Polish pronunciation hints rendered directly in the font — type Polish text and see Cyrillic or IPA phonetics above each digraph.
fonts.placeholder: Type Polish text here... try: szczegolnie, przyczyna, dziecko
fonts.sampleText: Przeczytaj szczegolnie o dzieciach z Lodzi. Czlowiek szedl przez rzeke, szukajac zrodla. Ciasto z orzechami i szynka na kolacje.
fonts.testHint: Try: szczegol, przyczyna, dziecko, czlowiek, szynka, zrodlo, ciasto
fonts.ipaNote: IPA uses correct retroflex symbols (ʂ ʐ tʂ dʐ) for Polish sz/rz/cz/dż. Affricate tie bars omitted for readability at small sizes.
fonts.teaserText: We also made fonts that show how to pronounce Polish — Cyrillic hints for Russian speakers, IPA for everyone else.

try.title: Try on any website
try.subtitle: Install one of three browser helpers and Szpargalka Sans takes over on every Polish page you visit, rendering inline pronunciation hints above the digraphs — shown here on the Polish Wikipedia article about the Polish language.
try.screenshotAltLight: Polish Wikipedia article about the Polish language rendered with Szpargalka Sans in light mode, showing Cyrillic pronunciation hints above Polish digraphs.
try.screenshotAltDark: The same Polish Wikipedia article rendered with Szpargalka Sans in Wikipedia's native dark mode.
try.chromeIntro: One-click download of the pre-built extension zip. Recommended path — it has a popup with per-site toggles and bundles the font as a base64 data URI so it works offline.
try.chromeStep2: Unzip it anywhere on your disk.
try.chromeStep3: Open chrome://extensions and enable Developer mode in the top-right corner.
try.chromeStep4: Click Load unpacked and point it at the unzipped chrome-mv3 folder. The extension ships disabled — click the toolbar icon to flip the global toggle to On, or leave it Off and use per-site overrides only.
try.tampermonkeyIntro: For power users who already run Tampermonkey. Single user-script, toggle modes through the Tampermonkey menu.
try.tampermonkeyStep1: Install the Tampermonkey extension if you don't have it.
try.tampermonkeyStep3: The script ships disabled. Click the Tampermonkey icon → hover Szpargalka Sans → click the entry to flip it On; click again to turn it back off. A ✓ marks the current state.
try.stylusIntro: The lightest path — pure user-CSS, no JavaScript. Works in any Stylus-compatible browser extension.
try.stylusStep1: Install the Stylus extension for your browser.
try.stylusStep3: Stylus opens the import dialog — click Install style. Done. Toggle the style on or off at any time from the Stylus popup — it ships enabled after install, so click it off if you only want to reach for it occasionally.
